content.text: Risk Owner Assignment and Mitigation Tracking

Identifying a risk and scoring it is necessary but not sufficient — a risk register without accountable owners and trackable mitigation tasks is a compliance document that nobody acts on. Effective AI risk management requires a clear ownership model (who is accountable for each risk), a defined mitigation lifecycle (what states a task moves through from identification to closure), and time-bound SLAs that trigger escalation when deadlines slip.

attrs.alt: Risk ownership and mitigation tracking RACI and lifecycle

content.text: Three Lines of Defence
Enterprise risk frameworks separate risk management into three organisational lines, each with a distinct ownership role in the AI risk register:
Line
Role in AI Risk
Typical Teams
Register Role
1st Line
Day-to-day risk ownership; implements and operates controls
RAG platform team, ML engineers, product managers
Accountable owner of each risk entry
2nd Line
Sets AI risk policy; reviews control evidence; challenges 1st line
AI Risk Office, Legal, Data Governance, CISO function
Risk Steward; validates EVIDENCE → VALIDATED transitions
3rd Line
Independent assurance; validates that the system actually works as claimed
Internal Audit, External Auditors
Annual review; reads the register but does not own entries

The separation matters because it prevents the same team from both implementing a control and declaring it effective. The RAG platform team deploys citation grounding; the AI Risk Office reviews the eval results to confirm it works.
RACI for Risk Entries
Every risk register entry carries a RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) map so each role is unambiguous:
RACI Role
Definition
AI Risk Example
Accountable
Single person who answers for the outcome. Cannot be delegated.
RAG platform lead — owns the hallucination risk entry
Responsible
Does the implementation work. Can be a team.
RAG engineering team — builds the citation-grounding control
Consulted
Two-way: asked for expert input before decisions.
Legal team — consulted on regulatory impact assessment
Informed
One-way: receives status updates, no action required.
CISO — notified of all critical risks and their status
Mitigation Task Lifecycle
Each risk has a mitigation plan containing one or more tasks. Tasks move through a defined set of states with validated transitions:

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import uuid
from datetime import date, timedelta


class MitigationStatus(Enum):
    IDENTIFIED  = "identified"   # risk registered, no owner yet
    ASSIGNED    = "assigned"     # owner and due date set
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"  # controls being designed or deployed
    EVIDENCE    = "evidence"     # control deployed; evidence being collected
    VALIDATED   = "validated"    # 2nd line reviewed and approved evidence
    CLOSED      = "closed"       # risk residual accepted; no further action
    OVERDUE     = "overdue"      # passed due date without reaching VALIDATED


class OwnerRole(Enum):
    ACCOUNTABLE = "accountable"  # the risk owner; answers for the outcome (1st line)
    RESPONSIBLE = "responsible"  # does the work; may be a team, not a person
    CONSULTED   = "consulted"    # subject-matter expert; input required
    INFORMED    = "informed"     # receives status updates; no action required


@dataclass
class RiskOwner:
    name: str              # person or team identifier
    role: OwnerRole
    email: str
    escalation_email: Optional[str] = None  # manager, for overdue escalations


@dataclass
class MitigationTask:
    title: str
    description: str
    owners: list[RiskOwner]
    due_date: date
    status: MitigationStatus = MitigationStatus.ASSIGNED
    task_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: f"TASK-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6].upper()}")
    evidence_links: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    completion_date: Optional[date] = None

    def is_overdue(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> bool:
        check_date = as_of or date.today()
        return (
            self.status not in {MitigationStatus.VALIDATED, MitigationStatus.CLOSED}
            and check_date > self.due_date
        )

    def days_remaining(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> int:
        check_date = as_of or date.today()
        return (self.due_date - check_date).days

    def accountable_owner(self) -> Optional[RiskOwner]:
        for o in self.owners:
            if o.role == OwnerRole.ACCOUNTABLE:
                return o
        return None
Mitigation Plan and SLA Gates

The mitigation plan aggregates tasks for a risk and enforces SLA deadlines. Critical risks must have a mitigation plan in place within 30 days of identification and reach CLOSED status within 90 days:

SLA gates are not aspirational targets — they are escalation triggers. A critical risk that has no mitigation plan after 30 days automatically escalates to the CISO and the AI governance board. Include this in the risk policy document so owners know from day one that deadlines have consequences.
Tracking State Transitions

The tracker enforces valid state transitions so a task cannot jump from IDENTIFIED to CLOSED without passing through EVIDENCE and VALIDATED. The 2nd-line approval step (EVIDENCE → VALIDATED) is the critical gate: it prevents the 1st line from self-certifying that a control works.
Worked Example: Critical Hallucination Risk

tracker = MitigationTracker()

# AI-001: Hallucination — critical risk, plan due in 30 days, close in 90 days
plan = MitigationPlan(risk_id="AI-001", risk_rating="critical")

task1 = MitigationTask(
    title="Deploy citation-grounded prompting",
    description=(
        "Update system prompt to instruct the LLM to only assert claims present in "
        "retrieved context chunks. Run eval suite (200 QA pairs) to confirm "
        "hallucination rate drops below 2%."
    ),
    owners=[
        RiskOwner("rag-platform-team", OwnerRole.ACCOUNTABLE,
                  "rag-ops@example.com", "rag-lead@example.com"),
        RiskOwner("ai-risk-office", OwnerRole.INFORMED,
                  "ai-risk@example.com"),
    ],
    due_date=date(2024, 3, 15),
)

task2 = MitigationTask(
    title="Implement confidence threshold suppression",
    description=(
        "Add retrieval score gate: if max chunk score < 0.70, return "
        'a deflection message instead of an answer. Verify in A/B test '
        "that CSAT is not degraded for above-threshold queries."
    ),
    owners=[
        RiskOwner("rag-platform-team", OwnerRole.ACCOUNTABLE,
                  "rag-ops@example.com"),
        RiskOwner("ux-research", OwnerRole.CONSULTED,
                  "ux@example.com"),
    ],
    due_date=date(2024, 4, 1),
)

plan.tasks = [task1, task2]
tracker.add_plan(plan)

# Simulate progress
tracker.advance_task("AI-001", task1.task_id, MitigationStatus.IN_PROGRESS)
tracker.advance_task(
    "AI-001", task1.task_id, MitigationStatus.EVIDENCE,
    evidence_link="https://confluence.example.com/eval-run-2024-03-10",
)
tracker.advance_task("AI-001", task1.task_id, MitigationStatus.VALIDATED)
tracker.advance_task("AI-001", task1.task_id, MitigationStatus.CLOSED)

print(f"Plan status: {plan.overall_status().value}")
# → in_progress (task2 still assigned)
print(f"Dashboard: {tracker.dashboard()}")
Overdue Escalation
A nightly job flags tasks whose due date has passed and sends an escalation email to the accountable owner's manager. This converts the SLA from a policy statement into an operational reality:

def escalate_overdue(tracker: MitigationTracker, as_of: date | None = None) -> None:
    """
    Runs nightly. Flags overdue tasks and sends escalation emails to the
    accountable owner's manager.
    """
    overdue_pairs = tracker.flag_overdue(as_of)
    for risk_id, task in overdue_pairs:
        owner = task.accountable_owner()
        if owner and owner.escalation_email:
            _send_escalation_email(
                to=owner.escalation_email,
                cc=owner.email,
                risk_id=risk_id,
                task=task,
            )

def _send_escalation_email(to: str, cc: str, risk_id: str, task: MitigationTask) -> None:
    # In practice: Jira ticket update or email via SES / SendGrid
    print(
        f"[ESCALATION] {risk_id} / {task.task_id} | "
        f"to={to} cc={cc} | "
        f"overdue by {abs(task.days_remaining())} days: {task.title}"
    )
Evidence Requirements by Control Type
When a task moves to the EVIDENCE state, the 2nd-line validator needs specific artefacts. Generic evidence ("control deployed") is not sufficient:
Control Type
Required Evidence
Rejection Criteria
Citation-grounded prompting
Eval suite run report: hallucination rate before and after, with 200+ QA pairs
Eval run < 100 items; no baseline comparison; rate stays above threshold
Confidence threshold suppression
A/B test results: suppression rate, CSAT delta, recall@5 comparison
No A/B test; only manual spot-checks; CSAT not measured
PII output scanner
Penetration test report: 50 adversarial prompts, 0 PII leakages; FP rate
Test coverage < 50 prompts; no adversarial prompts; FP rate > 5%
Role-based retrieval access control
IAM audit log: confirmed that cross-role retrieval requests return 0 results
Tested only with dev accounts; no audit log evidence
Human-in-the-loop review
Process documentation + sample review logs for 30-day period
No process documented; review logs not retained; coverage < 100%
Register Review Cadence
Risk entries are living documents. The review cadence is driven by the current residual rating:
Critical
: weekly review by the risk owner; monthly review with the 2nd line; board visibility at every quarterly AI governance meeting.
High
: bi-weekly status update from the risk owner; 2nd-line review every 6 weeks.
Medium
: monthly owner update; quarterly 2nd-line review.
Low
: quarterly review; the owner confirms the risk is still correctly rated and the controls are still active.

When any control that contributes to a residual score is modified, removed, or found to be less effective than assumed (e.g., a red-team exercise reveals a control bypass), the risk owner must re-rate the risk within 5 business days. This triggers the mitigation lifecycle again if the new residual rating is higher.
Integration with the Development Lifecycle
Risk ownership integrates with standard engineering workflows:
Architecture review
: for any new AI feature, the 1st-line team completes a risk intake form that populates draft entries in the register before the feature goes to production.
PR gates
: CI checks that any change to a prompt template, embedding model, or retrieval configuration links to an updated risk assessment. The check fails if the linked risk entry is OVERDUE.
Deployment checklist
: the deployment runbook includes a step confirming that all CRITICAL and HIGH risks for the feature are in VALIDATED or CLOSED state.
Incident linkage

: when a production incident occurs, it is linked to the relevant risk register entry. If no entry exists, the incident triggers a new intake. The incident resolution is treated as evidence for the relevant mitigation task.
Summary
Every risk entry has exactly one accountable owner in the 1st line; the 2nd line validates control evidence; the 3rd line provides independent assurance.
Mitigation tasks move through a defined lifecycle (IDENTIFIED → ASSIGNED → IN_PROGRESS → EVIDENCE → VALIDATED → CLOSED) with enforced valid transitions.
The EVIDENCE → VALIDATED gate requires 2nd-line approval — the 1st line cannot self-certify that a control works.
SLA deadlines (30 days to plan, 90 to close for critical risks) are escalation triggers, not aspirational targets.
Evidence requirements are specific per control type: eval suite reports, A/B test results, penetration test reports — not 'control deployed'.
Risk entries are re-rated whenever a contributing control is modified or found to be less effective than assumed.
